volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Truculentus (Section 2.1.220 - Section 2.3.325)

sections.label: Section 2.1.220
sections.original: nos divitem istum meminimus atque iste pauperes nos :
sections.translation: We remember him as rich , and he us as poor ; our reminiscences have shifted places . He must be a fool to wonder at it . If he is in want , it ’ s necessary that he should allow us to make a living ; that ’ s proper to be done . ’ Twere a disgrace for us to have compassion on men that squander away their fortunes . A clever Procuress ought to have good teeth ;

sections.label: Section 2.1.225
sections.original: adripere ut quisquis veniat blandeque adloqui ,
sections.translation: to smile upon whoever comes , to address him in flattering terms ; to design mischief in her heart , but to speak fairly with her tongue . A Courtesan it befits to be like a briar ; whatever man she touches , for either mischief or loss certainly to be the result . A Courtesan ought never to listen to the plea of a lover ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.230
sections.original: quin , ubi nil det , pro infrequente eum mittat militia domum .
sections.translation: but , when he has nothing to give , do you pack him off home from service as a deserter [*] ; and never is any gallant good for anything unless he ’ s one who is the enemy of his own fortune . It ’ s trifling , if , when he has just given , he doesn ’ t take a pleasure in giving afresh . That person ’ s esteemed with us who forgets that that has been given which he has given . As long as he has anything , so long let him go on loving ; when he has got nothing , then let him look out another employment ;

sections.label: Section 2.1.235
sections.original: aequo animo , ipse si nihil habeat , aliis quí habent , det locum .
sections.translation: if he himself has got nothing , let him , with a contented mind , make way for others who have . He ’ s a proper lover who , neglecting his affairs , squanders away his property . But among themselves the men declare that we act ill , and are greedy . Prithee , do we in fact at all act ill ? For , by my troth , never did any lover whatever give enough to his mistress ;

sections.label: Section 2.1.240
sections.original: neque pol nos satis accepimus neque umquam úlla satis poposcit .
sections.translation: nor , i ’ faith , have we ever received enough , nor has any woman ever asked for enough . For when a gallant is barren with his gifts ⋯ If he denies that he has anything to give , alone ⋯ Nor do we receive enough , when a person has not enough to give us . It is ever our duty to look after fresh givers ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.245
sections.original: qui de thensauris integris demus danunt .
sections.translation: who take from untouched treasures , and make presents to us . Just like this young man from the country , who dwells here ( pointing to the house where STRABAX lives ) , i ’ faith , a very pleasant creature , and a very bounteous giver . But he , without the knowledge of his father , even this very last night , leapt over the wall by way of the garden , and came to our house . I wish to meet with him .

sections.label: Section 2.1.250
sections.original: sed est huic unus servos violentissimus ,
sections.translation: But one servant has he , a very great savage , who , when he sees any one of us near the door , if you approach that way , drives us off just as he scares the geese away with his noise from the corn ; he ’ s such a bumpkin . But come what may , I ’ ll knock at the door .

sections.label: Section 2.1.254_255
sections.original: ecquis huíc tutelam ianuae gerit ? ecquis intus exit ?
sections.translation: ( Knocks at the door , and calls .) Who , I wonder , has the keeping of this door ? Is anybody coming out from in-doors ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.256
sections.original: Quís illic est qui tam proterve nostras aedis arietat ?
sections.translation: Who ’ s this [*] , that ’ s so sturdily plying his batteringram against our door ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.257
sections.original: Ego sum , respice ad me .
sections.translation: It ’ s I . Look round at me .

sections.label: Section 2.2.257b
sections.original: Quid ego ? nonne egó videor tibi ?
sections.translation: Who ’ s I ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.258
sections.original: quid tibi ad hasce accessio aedis est prope aut pultatio ?
sections.translation: Woe worth thee ! What mean you by this coming so near this door , or why ’ s this knocking ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.259
sections.original: Salve .
sections.translation: Health to you .

sections.label: Section 2.2.259b
sections.original: Sat mihi est tuae salutis . nil moror . non salveo ?
sections.translation: Enow of thy health have I ; I care nought for ’ t , I ’ ve got no health ;

sections.label: Section 2.2.260
sections.original: aegrotare malim quam esse tua salute sanior .
sections.translation: I ’ d rather be sick , than be a bit the sounder with health from thee . This I want to know , what ’ s owing thee here in our house ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.262
sections.original: Comprime sis eiram .
sections.translation: Do keep close —

sections.label: Section 2.2.262b
sections.original: Eam quidem hercle tu , quae solita es , comprime ,
sections.translation: Yea , faith , to my own good woman I trust ; let him keep close to thee whose habit ’ tis . A rare fine joke ! a silly hussy to be tempting a countryman to naughty tricks .

sections.label: Section 2.2.264
sections.original: Eiram dixi : ut decepisti ! dempsisti unam litteram .
sections.translation: Keep close your anger , I meant .

sections.label: Section 2.2.265
sections.original: nimis quidem hic truculentust .
sections.translation: Really this fellow ’ s very churlish .

sections.label: Section 2.2.265b
sections.original: Pergin male loqui , mulier , mihi ?
sections.translation: Woman , dost thee go on abusing me ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.266
sections.original: Quid tibi ego male dicó ?
sections.translation: Why , what did I say to you ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.266b
sections.original: Quia enim me truncum lentum nominas .
sections.translation: Why , because thee dost call me churlish . Therefore now , if thee doesn ’ t be off this instant , and tell me quickly what thee want ’ st , adzookers , woman , I ’ ll be , here this very instant , trampling thee beneath my feet like a sow her piglings .

sections.label: Section 2.2.269
sections.original: Rus merum hoc quidemst .
sections.translation: This is indeed right country ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.269b
sections.original: Pudendumst vero clurinum pecus .
sections.translation: and no mistake ; ’ tis an abominable and truly a monkey race .

sections.label: Section 2.2.270
sections.original: advenisti huc te ostentatum , cum exornatis ossibus ,
sections.translation: Hast thee come hither to tempt me with thy decked out bones [*] ? Was it for that , shameless slut , thee dyed thy mantle of its smoke-dried colour , or art thee so fine because that thee ’ s been a stealing ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.273
sections.original: Nunc places , cum mi inclementer dicis .
sections.translation: Now you charm me .

sections.label: Section 2.2.273b
sections.original: Quid hoc quod te rogo ?
sections.translation: What I ask thee . Dost thee wish to be taken for a bondswoman , who dost carry on thee those rings ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.275
sections.original: pignus da ni ligneae haec sunt quas habes Victorias .
sections.translation: They give them to those who are worthy .

sections.label: Section 2.2.276
sections.original: Ne attigas me .
sections.translation: Don ’ t be touching me .

sections.label: Section 2.2.276b
sections.original: Egon te tangam ? íta me amabit sarculum ,
sections.translation: I , touch thee ? So help me my weeding-hoe , I ’ d rather i ’ the country for me to be harnessed like an ox with crumpled horns , and with it spend the livelong night upon the straw , than that a hundred nights with thee , with a dinner apiece , were given me for nothing !

sections.label: Section 2.2.280
sections.original: rus tu mi opprobras ? ut nancta es hominem quem pudeat probri !
sections.translation: Dost thee throw the country in my teeth , when thee hast found a man who ’ s ashamed of what ’ s foul ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.281
sections.original: sed quid ápud nostras negoti , mulier , est aedis tibi ?
sections.translation: But what business , woman , hast thee at our house ? Why dost thee come running this way as often as we come to town ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.283
sections.original: Mulieres volo convenire vostras .
sections.translation: I want to meet with your women .

sections.label: Section 2.2.283b
sections.original: Quas tu mulieres
sections.translation: What women art thee talking to me about , when there ’ s not even a single woman-fly within the house ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.285
sections.original: Nullan istic mulier habitat ?
sections.translation: What , does no woman live here ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.285b
sections.original: Rus , inquam , abierunt . abi .
sections.translation: They ’ ve gone into the country , I say . Be off .

sections.label: Section 2.2.286
sections.original: Quid clamas , insane ?
sections.translation: Why are you bawling out , you lunatic ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.286b
sections.original: Abire hinc ni properas grandi gradu ,
sections.translation: If thee doesn ’ t make haste to get away from this with prodigious speed , I ’ ll forthwith be separating even from thy brains those falsified , daintily arranged , corkscrew curls of thine , with all their grease as well .

sections.label: Section 2.2.288b
sections.original: Quanam gratia ?
sections.translation: For what reason , pray ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.289
sections.original: Quia ad foris nostras unguentis uncta es ausa accedere
sections.translation: Why , because thee hast even presumed to approach our door anointed up with thine unguents , and because thee hast those cheeks so nicely , painted pink .

sections.label: Section 2.2.291
sections.original: Erubui mecastor misera propter clamorem tuom .
sections.translation: I ’ troth , it was by reason of your clamour that I coloured in my alarm .

sections.label: Section 2.2.292
sections.original: Itane ? erubuisti ? quasi vero corpori reliqueris
sections.translation: And is it so ? Thee coloured ? As though , hussy , thee really hadst left to thy skin the power of receiving any colour . Redden up thy cheeks , thee hast given all thy skin its colour with chalk [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.2.295
sections.original: pessumae estis .
sections.translation: Ye are scoundrelly jades .

sections.label: Section 2.2.295b
sections.original: Quíd est quod vobis pessumae haec male fecerint ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the reason , abominable hussies , that this way ⋯ ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.296
sections.original: Scio ego plus quam tu arbitrare scire me .
sections.translation: I know more than thee think ’ st I know .

sections.label: Section 2.2.296b
sections.original: Quid id obsecrost
sections.translation: Prithee , what ’ s this that you know ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.297b
sections.original: Erilis noster filius apud vos Strabax
sections.translation: How Strabax , my master ’ s son , is ruining himself at your house ; how you are all enticing him to fraud and present-making .

sections.label: Section 2.2.299
sections.original: Sanus si videare , dicam : dicis contumeliam .
sections.translation: If you appeared in your senses , I ’ d tell you . You ’ re uttering abuse only ;

sections.label: Section 2.2.300
sections.original: nemo homo hic solet perire apud nos : res perdunt suas ;
sections.translation: not a person is in the habit of being ruined here at our house ; they waste their property ; when they ’ ve wasted their property , they may go bare thence , if they choose . I don ’ t know this young man of yours .

sections.label: Section 2.2.302b
sections.original: Veron serio ?
sections.translation: Indeed so .

sections.label: Section 2.2.303
sections.original: quid maceria illa ait , in horto quae est , quae in noctes singulas
sections.translation: Aye , but that garden wall [*] that ’ s in our garden says so , which is becoming every night less by a brick , over which he travels to your house on the road to destruction .

sections.label: Section 2.2.305
sections.original: Nil mirum ( vetus est maceria ) lateres si veteres ruont .
sections.translation: The wall ’ s an old one ; it isn ’ t wonderful if the bricks , being old , do tumble down .

sections.label: Section 2.2.306
sections.original: Ain tu vero veteres lateres ruere ? numquam edepol mihi
sections.translation: And says thee , hussy , that old bricks do tumble down ? By my fakes , may never any mortal man henceforth trust me upon the two grand points [*] , if I don ’ t inform of these goings on of yours to my elder master .

sections.label: Section 2.2.309
sections.original: Estne item violentus ut tu ?
sections.translation: Is he a savage as well ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.309b
sections.original: Non enim ille meretriculis
sections.translation: Why , he didn ’ t get his money by enriching harlots , but by thriftiness ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.311
sections.original: duritiaque : quae nunc ad vos clam exportantur , pessumae ;
sections.translation: and living hard ; which now , however , is being carried off to you , abominable jades . ( Takes her by the shoulders and shakes her .) There ’ s for thee , six-clawed hussy ; a wretched life to both of ye . Am I to keep mum about these matters ? But , lookye now , I ’ ll be off to the Forum at once and tell these goings on to the old gentleman , that he mayn ’ t somehow be cherishing within this matting [*] a whole swarm of misfortunes .

sections.label: Section 2.2.315
sections.original: Si ecastor hic homo sinapi victitet , non censeam
sections.translation: Upon my faith , if this fellow were living on mustard , I don ’ t think he could possibly be as snappish . But , i ’ troth , how much a well-wisher to his master he is . Still , although he is a savage , I trust that he can be changed by coaxing , allurements , and other arts of the courtesan . I ’ ve seen a horse from unruly become tamed , and other brutes as well .

sections.label: Section 2.2.320
sections.original: nunc ad eram revidebo . sed eccum ódium progreditur meum .
sections.translation: Now I ’ ll go back to see my mistress . But see , my plague ’ s coming out . ( The door of PHRONESIUM ’ S house is opened .) He ’ s coming out with a sad air ; he hasn ’ t even yet had a meeting with Phronesium .

sections.label: Section 2.3.322
sections.original: Piscis ego credo , qui usque dum vivont lavant ,
sections.translation: I do believe that the fishes , that are always bathing as long as they live , do not take so long in bathing as this Phronesium does in bathing . If women could be loved on as long as [*] they take in bathing ,

sections.label: Section 2.3.325
sections.original: omnes amantes balneatores sient .
sections.translation: all lovers would be becoming bath-keepers .
